text: The following events were reported: Hail, Thunderstorm Wind, Tornado. These occurred near station USW00003927, approximately 31.721 km away, between 2017-03-29 00:50:00 and 2017-03-29 01:50:00. The events included records with hailstones measuring 56.56521739130435 inches.The events resulted in 0 injuries and 2 fatalities. The events caused property damage valued at 1235000.0 and crop damage of 0.0. Episode Narrative: A line of thunderstorms developed late in the day on the 28th and moved through much of north Texas during the early morning hours of the 29th. While hail and damaging wind gusts were the primary severe weather events, several QLCS tornadoes were observed as well. A line of thunderstorms developed late in the day on the 28th and moved through much of north Texas during the early morning hours of the 29th. Event Narrative: A trained spotter measured a 70 MPH wind gust 4 miles south-southwest of Richland Hills, TX. Mansfield Office of Emergency Management measured a wind gust of 85 MPH. A trained spotter reported quarter-sized hail on Hwy 720 north of Little Elm, TX. Denton County Fire Dispatch reported a tractor-trailer blown over by thunderstorm winds. Amateur radio reported 8 inch diameter limbs blown down in northeast Denton. The Denton, TX Automated Surface Observation System reported a 62 MPH wind gust. Emergency management reported a 10-inch diameter tree snapped in the town of Krugerville, TX. Wind gusts, estimated to be 80 mph damaged large trees and roofs in Keller. The wind damaged several large tree limbs and branches, and destroyed at least four roofs. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado caused damage to several areas in Fort Worth, Keller, and the far western portions of Southlake. This tornado occurred in a broader area of thunderstorm winds, yet a convergent path of damage was noted in Keller, especially near US 377 and North Tarrant Parkway. Several homes suffered minor roof damage, while fence and tree damage was most common. One business suffered damage to their roof and several awnings. An off duty National Weather Service employee measured an 83 MPH in north Fort Worth. A broadcast media meteorologist measured a wind gust of 62 mph in Keller. Power lines blown down. The power lines remained active Wednesday, resulting in the electrocution of 2 brothers playing in the area. A trained spotter estimated 75 MPH winds in the city of Frisco, TX. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado produced EF-0 damage in north Fort Worth and Haslet areas. The tornado was embedded in a line of thunderstorms, yet a distinct convergent path was noted for the entire life of this tornado. The tornado began near US 287 and Bonds Ranch Road, before moving north and northeast. The tornado damaged mainly trees and billboards until it crossed Interstate 35W near Timberland Boulevard. Several trees, fences, and approximately 50 roofs suffered damage in housing developments along and north of Timberland. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado produced EF-1 damage in the city of Lewisville. The damage began near the intersection of Fox avenue and Boxwood, before moving to the north and northeast, ending near I-35E. Severe damage was done to about ten homes, with 5 homes losing part of the roof, along with blown out windows. The damage was most concentrated at the beginning of the tornado, yet several businesses and government buildings suffered damage as the storm quickly moved through part of Lewisville. A social media report indicated that several fences were blown down in the city of Frisco, TX. A public report indicated large tree limbs down and minor roof damage in the Eagle Mountain area. A trained spotter reported trees down, metal buildings destroyed and roof damage to multiple structures in the vicinity of Meadow Glen Drive in Crowley, TX. Emergency management reported large trees snapped in half and a barn blown into a pond near the town of Rendon. Emergency management reported roof damage at the fire station at 4416 Southwest Blvd in Fort Worth, TX. Emergency management reported trees down throughout the city of Mansfield, TX. A social media report indicated roof damage at Household of Faith Church in Arlington, TX. A social media report indicated downed communications antennas at Collins St and Sublett Rd in Arlington, TX. Broadcast media reported that one plane was damaged at Arlington Municipal Airport, and large trees were broken near the base of the trunks. A social media report indicated that several sections of brick wall were blown over near the intersection of Ponselle Dr and Matlock Rd in Arlington, TX. A 58 MPH wind gust was measured at the NWS office in Fort Worth, TX.

Event Narrative: A trained spotter measured a 70 MPH wind gust 4 miles south-southwest of Richland Hills, TX. Mansfield Office of Emergency Management measured a wind gust of 85 MPH. A trained spotter reported quarter-sized hail on Hwy 720 north of Little Elm, TX. Denton County Fire Dispatch reported a tractor-trailer blown over by thunderstorm winds. Amateur radio reported 8 inch diameter limbs blown down in northeast Denton. The Denton, TX Automated Surface Observation System reported a 62 MPH wind gust. Emergency management reported a 10-inch diameter tree snapped in the town of Krugerville, TX. Wind gusts, estimated to be 80 mph damaged large trees and roofs in Keller. The wind damaged several large tree limbs and branches, and destroyed at least four roofs. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado caused damage to several areas in Fort Worth, Keller, and the far western portions of Southlake. This tornado occurred in a broader area of thunderstorm winds, yet a convergent path of damage was noted in Keller, especially near US 377 and North Tarrant Parkway. Several homes suffered minor roof damage, while fence and tree damage was most common. One business suffered damage to their roof and several awnings. An off duty National Weather Service employee measured an 83 MPH in north Fort Worth. A broadcast media meteorologist measured a wind gust of 62 mph in Keller. Power lines blown down. The power lines remained active Wednesday, resulting in the electrocution of 2 brothers playing in the area. A trained spotter estimated 75 MPH winds in the city of Frisco, TX. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado produced EF-0 damage in north Fort Worth and Haslet areas. The tornado was embedded in a line of thunderstorms, yet a distinct convergent path was noted for the entire life of this tornado. The tornado began near US 287 and Bonds Ranch Road, before moving north and northeast. The tornado damaged mainly trees and billboards until it crossed Interstate 35W near Timberland Boulevard. Several trees, fences, and approximately 50 roofs suffered damage in housing developments along and north of Timberland. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado produced EF-1 damage in the city of Lewisville. The damage began near the intersection of Fox avenue and Boxwood, before moving to the north and northeast, ending near I-35E. Severe damage was done to about ten homes, with 5 homes losing part of the roof, along with blown out windows. The damage was most concentrated at the beginning of the tornado, yet several businesses and government buildings suffered damage as the storm quickly moved through part of Lewisville. A social media report indicated that several fences were blown down in the city of Frisco, TX. A public report indicated large tree limbs down and minor roof damage in the Eagle Mountain area. A trained spotter reported trees down, metal buildings destroyed and roof damage to multiple structures in the vicinity of Meadow Glen Drive in Crowley, TX. Emergency management reported large trees snapped in half and a barn blown into a pond near the town of Rendon. Emergency management reported roof damage at the fire station at 4416 Southwest Blvd in Fort Worth, TX. Emergency management reported trees down throughout the city of Mansfield, TX. A social media report indicated roof damage at Household of Faith Church in Arlington, TX. A social media report indicated downed communications antennas at Collins St and Sublett Rd in Arlington, TX. Broadcast media reported that one plane was damaged at Arlington Municipal Airport, and large trees were broken near the base of the trunks. A social media report indicated that several sections of brick wall were blown over near the intersection of Ponselle Dr and Matlock Rd in Arlington, TX. A 58 MPH wind gust was measured at the NWS office in Fort Worth, TX.

Emergency management reported a 10-inch diameter tree snapped in the town of Krugerville, TX. Wind gusts, estimated to be 80 mph damaged large trees and roofs in Keller. The wind damaged several large tree limbs and branches, and destroyed at least four roofs. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado caused damage to several areas in Fort Worth, Keller, and the far western portions of Southlake. This tornado occurred in a broader area of thunderstorm winds, yet a convergent path of damage was noted in Keller, especially near US 377 and North Tarrant Parkway. Several homes suffered minor roof damage, while fence and tree damage was most common. One business suffered damage to their roof and several awnings. An off duty National Weather Service employee measured an 83 MPH in north Fort Worth. A broadcast media meteorologist measured a wind gust of 62 mph in Keller. Power lines blown down. The power lines remained active Wednesday, resulting in the electrocution of 2 brothers playing in the area. A trained spotter estimated 75 MPH winds in the city of Frisco, TX. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado produced EF-0 damage in north Fort Worth and Haslet areas. The tornado was embedded in a line of thunderstorms, yet a distinct convergent path was noted for the entire life of this tornado. The tornado began near US 287 and Bonds Ranch Road, before moving north and northeast. The tornado damaged mainly trees and billboards until it crossed Interstate 35W near Timberland Boulevard. Several trees, fences, and approximately 50 roofs suffered damage in housing developments along and north of Timberland. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado produced EF-1 damage in the city of Lewisville. The damage began near the intersection of Fox avenue and Boxwood, before moving to the north and northeast, ending near I-35E. Severe damage was done to about ten homes, with 5 homes losing part of the roof, along with blown out windows. The damage was most concentrated at the beginning of the tornado, yet several businesses and government buildings suffered damage as the storm quickly moved through part of Lewisville. A social media report indicated that several fences were blown down in the city of Frisco, TX. A public report indicated large tree limbs down and minor roof damage in the Eagle Mountain area. A trained spotter reported trees down, metal buildings destroyed and roof damage to multiple structures in the vicinity of Meadow Glen Drive in Crowley, TX. Emergency management reported large trees snapped in half and a barn blown into a pond near the town of Rendon. Emergency management reported roof damage at the fire station at 4416 Southwest Blvd in Fort Worth, TX. Emergency management reported trees down throughout the city of Mansfield, TX. A social media report indicated roof damage at Household of Faith Church in Arlington, TX. A social media report indicated downed communications antennas at Collins St and Sublett Rd in Arlington, TX. Broadcast media reported that one plane was damaged at Arlington Municipal Airport, and large trees were broken near the base of the trunks. A social media report indicated that several sections of brick wall were blown over near the intersection of Ponselle Dr and Matlock Rd in Arlington, TX. A 58 MPH wind gust was measured at the NWS office in Fort Worth, TX.

The tornado began near US 287 and Bonds Ranch Road, before moving north and northeast. The tornado damaged mainly trees and billboards until it crossed Interstate 35W near Timberland Boulevard. Several trees, fences, and approximately 50 roofs suffered damage in housing developments along and north of Timberland. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado produced EF-1 damage in the city of Lewisville. The damage began near the intersection of Fox avenue and Boxwood, before moving to the north and northeast, ending near I-35E. Severe damage was done to about ten homes, with 5 homes losing part of the roof, along with blown out windows. The damage was most concentrated at the beginning of the tornado, yet several businesses and government buildings suffered damage as the storm quickly moved through part of Lewisville. A social media report indicated that several fences were blown down in the city of Frisco, TX. A public report indicated large tree limbs down and minor roof damage in the Eagle Mountain area. A trained spotter reported trees down, metal buildings destroyed and roof damage to multiple structures in the vicinity of Meadow Glen Drive in Crowley, TX. Emergency management reported large trees snapped in half and a barn blown into a pond near the town of Rendon. Emergency management reported roof damage at the fire station at 4416 Southwest Blvd in Fort Worth, TX. Emergency management reported trees down throughout the city of Mansfield, TX. A social media report indicated roof damage at Household of Faith Church in Arlington, TX. A social media report indicated downed communications antennas at Collins St and Sublett Rd in Arlington, TX. Broadcast media reported that one plane was damaged at Arlington Municipal Airport, and large trees were broken near the base of the trunks. A social media report indicated that several sections of brick wall were blown over near the intersection of Ponselle Dr and Matlock Rd in Arlington, TX. A 58 MPH wind gust was measured at the NWS office in Fort Worth, TX.

A line of thunderstorms developed late in the day on the 28th and moved through much of north Texas during the early morning hours of the 29th. Event Narrative: A trained spotter measured a 70 MPH wind gust 4 miles south-southwest of Richland Hills, TX. Mansfield Office of Emergency Management measured a wind gust of 85 MPH. A trained spotter reported quarter-sized hail on Hwy 720 north of Little Elm, TX. Denton County Fire Dispatch reported a tractor-trailer blown over by thunderstorm winds. Amateur radio reported 8 inch diameter limbs blown down in northeast Denton. The Denton, TX Automated Surface Observation System reported a 62 MPH wind gust. Emergency management reported a 10-inch diameter tree snapped in the town of Krugerville, TX. Wind gusts, estimated to be 80 mph damaged large trees and roofs in Keller. The wind damaged several large tree limbs and branches, and destroyed at least four roofs. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado caused damage to several areas in Fort Worth, Keller, and the far western portions of Southlake. This tornado occurred in a broader area of thunderstorm winds, yet a convergent path of damage was noted in Keller, especially near US 377 and North Tarrant Parkway. Several homes suffered minor roof damage, while fence and tree damage was most common. One business suffered damage to their roof and several awnings. An off duty National Weather Service employee measured an 83 MPH in north Fort Worth. A broadcast media meteorologist measured a wind gust of 62 mph in Keller. Power lines blown down. The power lines remained active Wednesday, resulting in the electrocution of 2 brothers playing in the area. A trained spotter estimated 75 MPH winds in the city of Frisco, TX. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado produced EF-0 damage in north Fort Worth and Haslet areas. The tornado was embedded in a line of thunderstorms, yet a distinct convergent path was noted for the entire life of this tornado. The tornado began near US 287 and Bonds Ranch Road, before moving north and northeast. The tornado damaged mainly trees and billboards until it crossed Interstate 35W near Timberland Boulevard. Several trees, fences, and approximately 50 roofs suffered damage in housing developments along and north of Timberland. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado produced EF-1 damage in the city of Lewisville. The damage began near the intersection of Fox avenue and Boxwood, before moving to the north and northeast, ending near I-35E. Severe damage was done to about ten homes, with 5 homes losing part of the roof, along with blown out windows. The damage was most concentrated at the beginning of the tornado, yet several businesses and government buildings suffered damage as the storm quickly moved through part of Lewisville. A social media report indicated that several fences were blown down in the city of Frisco, TX. A public report indicated large tree limbs down and minor roof damage in the Eagle Mountain area. A trained spotter reported trees down, metal buildings destroyed and roof damage to multiple structures in the vicinity of Meadow Glen Drive in Crowley, TX. Emergency management reported large trees snapped in half and a barn blown into a pond near the town of Rendon. Emergency management reported roof damage at the fire station at 4416 Southwest Blvd in Fort Worth, TX. Emergency management reported trees down throughout the city of Mansfield, TX. A social media report indicated roof damage at Household of Faith Church in Arlington, TX. A social media report indicated downed communications antennas at Collins St and Sublett Rd in Arlington, TX. Broadcast media reported that one plane was damaged at Arlington Municipal Airport, and large trees were broken near the base of the trunks. A social media report indicated that several sections of brick wall were blown over near the intersection of Ponselle Dr and Matlock Rd in Arlington, TX. A 58 MPH wind gust was measured at the NWS office in Fort Worth, TX.

Amateur radio reported 8 inch diameter limbs blown down in northeast Denton. The Denton, TX Automated Surface Observation System reported a 62 MPH wind gust. Emergency management reported a 10-inch diameter tree snapped in the town of Krugerville, TX. Wind gusts, estimated to be 80 mph damaged large trees and roofs in Keller. The wind damaged several large tree limbs and branches, and destroyed at least four roofs. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado caused damage to several areas in Fort Worth, Keller, and the far western portions of Southlake. This tornado occurred in a broader area of thunderstorm winds, yet a convergent path of damage was noted in Keller, especially near US 377 and North Tarrant Parkway. Several homes suffered minor roof damage, while fence and tree damage was most common. One business suffered damage to their roof and several awnings. An off duty National Weather Service employee measured an 83 MPH in north Fort Worth. A broadcast media meteorologist measured a wind gust of 62 mph in Keller. Power lines blown down. The power lines remained active Wednesday, resulting in the electrocution of 2 brothers playing in the area. A trained spotter estimated 75 MPH winds in the city of Frisco, TX. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado produced EF-0 damage in north Fort Worth and Haslet areas. The tornado was embedded in a line of thunderstorms, yet a distinct convergent path was noted for the entire life of this tornado. The tornado began near US 287 and Bonds Ranch Road, before moving north and northeast. The tornado damaged mainly trees and billboards until it crossed Interstate 35W near Timberland Boulevard. Several trees, fences, and approximately 50 roofs suffered damage in housing developments along and north of Timberland. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado produced EF-1 damage in the city of Lewisville. The damage began near the intersection of Fox avenue and Boxwood, before moving to the north and northeast, ending near I-35E. Severe damage was done to about ten homes, with 5 homes losing part of the roof, along with blown out windows. The damage was most concentrated at the beginning of the tornado, yet several businesses and government buildings suffered damage as the storm quickly moved through part of Lewisville. A social media report indicated that several fences were blown down in the city of Frisco, TX. A public report indicated large tree limbs down and minor roof damage in the Eagle Mountain area. A trained spotter reported trees down, metal buildings destroyed and roof damage to multiple structures in the vicinity of Meadow Glen Drive in Crowley, TX. Emergency management reported large trees snapped in half and a barn blown into a pond near the town of Rendon. Emergency management reported roof damage at the fire station at 4416 Southwest Blvd in Fort Worth, TX. Emergency management reported trees down throughout the city of Mansfield, TX. A social media report indicated roof damage at Household of Faith Church in Arlington, TX. A social media report indicated downed communications antennas at Collins St and Sublett Rd in Arlington, TX. Broadcast media reported that one plane was damaged at Arlington Municipal Airport, and large trees were broken near the base of the trunks. A social media report indicated that several sections of brick wall were blown over near the intersection of Ponselle Dr and Matlock Rd in Arlington, TX. A 58 MPH wind gust was measured at the NWS office in Fort Worth, TX.

A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado caused damage to several areas in Fort Worth, Keller, and the far western portions of Southlake. This tornado occurred in a broader area of thunderstorm winds, yet a convergent path of damage was noted in Keller, especially near US 377 and North Tarrant Parkway. Several homes suffered minor roof damage, while fence and tree damage was most common. One business suffered damage to their roof and several awnings. An off duty National Weather Service employee measured an 83 MPH in north Fort Worth. A broadcast media meteorologist measured a wind gust of 62 mph in Keller. Power lines blown down. The power lines remained active Wednesday, resulting in the electrocution of 2 brothers playing in the area. A trained spotter estimated 75 MPH winds in the city of Frisco, TX. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado produced EF-0 damage in north Fort Worth and Haslet areas. The tornado was embedded in a line of thunderstorms, yet a distinct convergent path was noted for the entire life of this tornado. The tornado began near US 287 and Bonds Ranch Road, before moving north and northeast. The tornado damaged mainly trees and billboards until it crossed Interstate 35W near Timberland Boulevard. Several trees, fences, and approximately 50 roofs suffered damage in housing developments along and north of Timberland. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado produced EF-1 damage in the city of Lewisville. The damage began near the intersection of Fox avenue and Boxwood, before moving to the north and northeast, ending near I-35E. Severe damage was done to about ten homes, with 5 homes losing part of the roof, along with blown out windows. The damage was most concentrated at the beginning of the tornado, yet several businesses and government buildings suffered damage as the storm quickly moved through part of Lewisville. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado produced EF-0 damage in north Fort Worth and Haslet areas. The tornado was embedded in a line of thunderstorms, yet a distinct convergent path was noted for the entire life of this tornado. The tornado began near US 287 and Bonds Ranch Road, before moving north and northeast. The tornado damaged mainly trees and billboards until it crossed Interstate 35W near Timberland Boulevard. Several trees, fences, and approximately 50 roofs suffered damage in housing developments along and north of Timberland. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado produced EF-1 damage in the city of Lewisville. The damage began near the intersection of Fox avenue and Boxwood, before moving to the north and northeast, ending near I-35E. Severe damage was done to about ten homes, with 5 homes losing part of the roof, along with blown out windows. The damage was most concentrated at the beginning of the tornado, yet several businesses and government buildings suffered damage as the storm quickly moved through part of Lewisville. A social media report indicated that several fences were blown down in the city of Frisco, TX. A public report indicated large tree limbs down and minor roof damage in the Eagle Mountain area. A trained spotter reported trees down, metal buildings destroyed and roof damage to multiple structures in the vicinity of Meadow Glen Drive in Crowley, TX. Emergency management reported large trees snapped in half and a barn blown into a pond near the town of Rendon. Emergency management reported roof damage at the fire station at 4416 Southwest Blvd in Fort Worth, TX. Emergency management reported trees down throughout the city of Mansfield, TX. A social media report indicated roof damage at Household of Faith Church in Arlington, TX. A social media report indicated downed communications antennas at Collins St and Sublett Rd in Arlington, TX. Broadcast media reported that one plane was damaged at Arlington Municipal Airport, and large trees were broken near the base of the trunks. A social media report indicated that several sections of brick wall were blown over near the intersection of Ponselle Dr and Matlock Rd in Arlington, TX. A 58 MPH wind gust was measured at the NWS office in Fort Worth, TX.

The events caused property damage valued at 1235000.0 and crop damage of 0.0. Episode Narrative: A line of thunderstorms developed late in the day on the 28th and moved through much of north Texas during the early morning hours of the 29th. While hail and damaging wind gusts were the primary severe weather events, several QLCS tornadoes were observed as well. A line of thunderstorms developed late in the day on the 28th and moved through much of north Texas during the early morning hours of the 29th. Event Narrative: A trained spotter measured a 70 MPH wind gust 4 miles south-southwest of Richland Hills, TX. Mansfield Office of Emergency Management measured a wind gust of 85 MPH. A trained spotter reported quarter-sized hail on Hwy 720 north of Little Elm, TX. Denton County Fire Dispatch reported a tractor-trailer blown over by thunderstorm winds. Amateur radio reported 8 inch diameter limbs blown down in northeast Denton. The Denton, TX Automated Surface Observation System reported a 62 MPH wind gust. Emergency management reported a 10-inch diameter tree snapped in the town of Krugerville, TX. Wind gusts, estimated to be 80 mph damaged large trees and roofs in Keller. The wind damaged several large tree limbs and branches, and destroyed at least four roofs. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado caused damage to several areas in Fort Worth, Keller, and the far western portions of Southlake. This tornado occurred in a broader area of thunderstorm winds, yet a convergent path of damage was noted in Keller, especially near US 377 and North Tarrant Parkway. Several homes suffered minor roof damage, while fence and tree damage was most common. One business suffered damage to their roof and several awnings. An off duty National Weather Service employee measured an 83 MPH in north Fort Worth. A broadcast media meteorologist measured a wind gust of 62 mph in Keller. Power lines blown down. The power lines remained active Wednesday, resulting in the electrocution of 2 brothers playing in the area. A trained spotter estimated 75 MPH winds in the city of Frisco, TX. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado produced EF-0 damage in north Fort Worth and Haslet areas. The tornado was embedded in a line of thunderstorms, yet a distinct convergent path was noted for the entire life of this tornado. The tornado began near US 287 and Bonds Ranch Road, before moving north and northeast. The tornado damaged mainly trees and billboards until it crossed Interstate 35W near Timberland Boulevard. Several trees, fences, and approximately 50 roofs suffered damage in housing developments along and north of Timberland. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado produced EF-1 damage in the city of Lewisville. The damage began near the intersection of Fox avenue and Boxwood, before moving to the north and northeast, ending near I-35E. Severe damage was done to about ten homes, with 5 homes losing part of the roof, along with blown out windows. The damage was most concentrated at the beginning of the tornado, yet several businesses and government buildings suffered damage as the storm quickly moved through part of Lewisville. A social media report indicated that several fences were blown down in the city of Frisco, TX. A public report indicated large tree limbs down and minor roof damage in the Eagle Mountain area. A trained spotter reported trees down, metal buildings destroyed and roof damage to multiple structures in the vicinity of Meadow Glen Drive in Crowley, TX. Emergency management reported large trees snapped in half and a barn blown into a pond near the town of Rendon. Emergency management reported roof damage at the fire station at 4416 Southwest Blvd in Fort Worth, TX. Emergency management reported trees down throughout the city of Mansfield, TX. A social media report indicated roof damage at Household of Faith Church in Arlington, TX. A social media report indicated downed communications antennas at Collins St and Sublett Rd in Arlington, TX. Broadcast media reported that one plane was damaged at Arlington Municipal Airport, and large trees were broken near the base of the trunks. A social media report indicated that several sections of brick wall were blown over near the intersection of Ponselle Dr and Matlock Rd in Arlington, TX. A 58 MPH wind gust was measured at the NWS office in Fort Worth, TX.

Event Narrative: A trained spotter measured a 70 MPH wind gust 4 miles south-southwest of Richland Hills, TX. Mansfield Office of Emergency Management measured a wind gust of 85 MPH. A trained spotter reported quarter-sized hail on Hwy 720 north of Little Elm, TX. Denton County Fire Dispatch reported a tractor-trailer blown over by thunderstorm winds. Amateur radio reported 8 inch diameter limbs blown down in northeast Denton. The Denton, TX Automated Surface Observation System reported a 62 MPH wind gust. Emergency management reported a 10-inch diameter tree snapped in the town of Krugerville, TX. Wind gusts, estimated to be 80 mph damaged large trees and roofs in Keller. The wind damaged several large tree limbs and branches, and destroyed at least four roofs. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado caused damage to several areas in Fort Worth, Keller, and the far western portions of Southlake. This tornado occurred in a broader area of thunderstorm winds, yet a convergent path of damage was noted in Keller, especially near US 377 and North Tarrant Parkway. Several homes suffered minor roof damage, while fence and tree damage was most common. One business suffered damage to their roof and several awnings. An off duty National Weather Service employee measured an 83 MPH in north Fort Worth. A broadcast media meteorologist measured a wind gust of 62 mph in Keller. Power lines blown down. The power lines remained active Wednesday, resulting in the electrocution of 2 brothers playing in the area. A trained spotter estimated 75 MPH winds in the city of Frisco, TX. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado produced EF-0 damage in north Fort Worth and Haslet areas. The tornado was embedded in a line of thunderstorms, yet a distinct convergent path was noted for the entire life of this tornado. The tornado began near US 287 and Bonds Ranch Road, before moving north and northeast. The tornado damaged mainly trees and billboards until it crossed Interstate 35W near Timberland Boulevard. Several trees, fences, and approximately 50 roofs suffered damage in housing developments along and north of Timberland. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado produced EF-1 damage in the city of Lewisville. The damage began near the intersection of Fox avenue and Boxwood, before moving to the north and northeast, ending near I-35E. Severe damage was done to about ten homes, with 5 homes losing part of the roof, along with blown out windows. The damage was most concentrated at the beginning of the tornado, yet several businesses and government buildings suffered damage as the storm quickly moved through part of Lewisville. A social media report indicated that several fences were blown down in the city of Frisco, TX. A public report indicated large tree limbs down and minor roof damage in the Eagle Mountain area. A trained spotter reported trees down, metal buildings destroyed and roof damage to multiple structures in the vicinity of Meadow Glen Drive in Crowley, TX. Emergency management reported large trees snapped in half and a barn blown into a pond near the town of Rendon. Emergency management reported roof damage at the fire station at 4416 Southwest Blvd in Fort Worth, TX. Emergency management reported trees down throughout the city of Mansfield, TX. A social media report indicated roof damage at Household of Faith Church in Arlington, TX. A social media report indicated downed communications antennas at Collins St and Sublett Rd in Arlington, TX. Broadcast media reported that one plane was damaged at Arlington Municipal Airport, and large trees were broken near the base of the trunks. A social media report indicated that several sections of brick wall were blown over near the intersection of Ponselle Dr and Matlock Rd in Arlington, TX. A 58 MPH wind gust was measured at the NWS office in Fort Worth, TX.

Emergency management reported a 10-inch diameter tree snapped in the town of Krugerville, TX. Wind gusts, estimated to be 80 mph damaged large trees and roofs in Keller. The wind damaged several large tree limbs and branches, and destroyed at least four roofs. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado caused damage to several areas in Fort Worth, Keller, and the far western portions of Southlake. This tornado occurred in a broader area of thunderstorm winds, yet a convergent path of damage was noted in Keller, especially near US 377 and North Tarrant Parkway. Several homes suffered minor roof damage, while fence and tree damage was most common. One business suffered damage to their roof and several awnings. An off duty National Weather Service employee measured an 83 MPH in north Fort Worth. A broadcast media meteorologist measured a wind gust of 62 mph in Keller. Power lines blown down. The power lines remained active Wednesday, resulting in the electrocution of 2 brothers playing in the area. A trained spotter estimated 75 MPH winds in the city of Frisco, TX. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado produced EF-0 damage in north Fort Worth and Haslet areas. The tornado was embedded in a line of thunderstorms, yet a distinct convergent path was noted for the entire life of this tornado. The tornado began near US 287 and Bonds Ranch Road, before moving north and northeast. The tornado damaged mainly trees and billboards until it crossed Interstate 35W near Timberland Boulevard. Several trees, fences, and approximately 50 roofs suffered damage in housing developments along and north of Timberland. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado produced EF-1 damage in the city of Lewisville. The damage began near the intersection of Fox avenue and Boxwood, before moving to the north and northeast, ending near I-35E. Severe damage was done to about ten homes, with 5 homes losing part of the roof, along with blown out windows. The damage was most concentrated at the beginning of the tornado, yet several businesses and government buildings suffered damage as the storm quickly moved through part of Lewisville. A social media report indicated that several fences were blown down in the city of Frisco, TX. A public report indicated large tree limbs down and minor roof damage in the Eagle Mountain area. A trained spotter reported trees down, metal buildings destroyed and roof damage to multiple structures in the vicinity of Meadow Glen Drive in Crowley, TX. Emergency management reported large trees snapped in half and a barn blown into a pond near the town of Rendon. Emergency management reported roof damage at the fire station at 4416 Southwest Blvd in Fort Worth, TX. Emergency management reported trees down throughout the city of Mansfield, TX. A social media report indicated roof damage at Household of Faith Church in Arlington, TX. A social media report indicated downed communications antennas at Collins St and Sublett Rd in Arlington, TX. Broadcast media reported that one plane was damaged at Arlington Municipal Airport, and large trees were broken near the base of the trunks. A social media report indicated that several sections of brick wall were blown over near the intersection of Ponselle Dr and Matlock Rd in Arlington, TX. A 58 MPH wind gust was measured at the NWS office in Fort Worth, TX.

The tornado began near US 287 and Bonds Ranch Road, before moving north and northeast. The tornado damaged mainly trees and billboards until it crossed Interstate 35W near Timberland Boulevard. Several trees, fences, and approximately 50 roofs suffered damage in housing developments along and north of Timberland. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado produced EF-1 damage in the city of Lewisville. The damage began near the intersection of Fox avenue and Boxwood, before moving to the north and northeast, ending near I-35E. Severe damage was done to about ten homes, with 5 homes losing part of the roof, along with blown out windows. The damage was most concentrated at the beginning of the tornado, yet several businesses and government buildings suffered damage as the storm quickly moved through part of Lewisville. A social media report indicated that several fences were blown down in the city of Frisco, TX. A public report indicated large tree limbs down and minor roof damage in the Eagle Mountain area. A trained spotter reported trees down, metal buildings destroyed and roof damage to multiple structures in the vicinity of Meadow Glen Drive in Crowley, TX. Emergency management reported large trees snapped in half and a barn blown into a pond near the town of Rendon. Emergency management reported roof damage at the fire station at 4416 Southwest Blvd in Fort Worth, TX. Emergency management reported trees down throughout the city of Mansfield, TX. A social media report indicated roof damage at Household of Faith Church in Arlington, TX. A social media report indicated downed communications antennas at Collins St and Sublett Rd in Arlington, TX. Broadcast media reported that one plane was damaged at Arlington Municipal Airport, and large trees were broken near the base of the trunks. A social media report indicated that several sections of brick wall were blown over near the intersection of Ponselle Dr and Matlock Rd in Arlington, TX. A 58 MPH wind gust was measured at the NWS office in Fort Worth, TX.

A line of thunderstorms developed late in the day on the 28th and moved through much of north Texas during the early morning hours of the 29th. Event Narrative: A trained spotter measured a 70 MPH wind gust 4 miles south-southwest of Richland Hills, TX. Mansfield Office of Emergency Management measured a wind gust of 85 MPH. A trained spotter reported quarter-sized hail on Hwy 720 north of Little Elm, TX. Denton County Fire Dispatch reported a tractor-trailer blown over by thunderstorm winds. Amateur radio reported 8 inch diameter limbs blown down in northeast Denton. The Denton, TX Automated Surface Observation System reported a 62 MPH wind gust. Emergency management reported a 10-inch diameter tree snapped in the town of Krugerville, TX. Wind gusts, estimated to be 80 mph damaged large trees and roofs in Keller. The wind damaged several large tree limbs and branches, and destroyed at least four roofs. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado caused damage to several areas in Fort Worth, Keller, and the far western portions of Southlake. This tornado occurred in a broader area of thunderstorm winds, yet a convergent path of damage was noted in Keller, especially near US 377 and North Tarrant Parkway. Several homes suffered minor roof damage, while fence and tree damage was most common. One business suffered damage to their roof and several awnings. An off duty National Weather Service employee measured an 83 MPH in north Fort Worth. A broadcast media meteorologist measured a wind gust of 62 mph in Keller. Power lines blown down. The power lines remained active Wednesday, resulting in the electrocution of 2 brothers playing in the area. A trained spotter estimated 75 MPH winds in the city of Frisco, TX. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado produced EF-0 damage in north Fort Worth and Haslet areas. The tornado was embedded in a line of thunderstorms, yet a distinct convergent path was noted for the entire life of this tornado. The tornado began near US 287 and Bonds Ranch Road, before moving north and northeast. The tornado damaged mainly trees and billboards until it crossed Interstate 35W near Timberland Boulevard. Several trees, fences, and approximately 50 roofs suffered damage in housing developments along and north of Timberland. A National Weather Service survey crew determined a tornado produced EF-1 damage in the city of Lewisville. The damage began near the intersection of Fox avenue and Boxwood, before moving to the north and northeast, ending near I-35E. Severe damage was done to about ten homes, with 5 homes losing part of the roof, along with blown out windows. The damage was most concentrated at the beginning of the tornado, yet several businesses and government buildings suffered damage as the storm quickly moved through part of Lewisville. A social media report indicated that several fences were blown down in the city of Frisco, TX. A public report indicated large tree limbs down and minor roof damage in the Eagle Mountain area. A trained spotter reported trees down, metal buildings destroyed and roof damage to multiple structures in the vicinity of Meadow Glen Drive in Crowley, TX. Emergency management reported large trees snapped in half and a barn blown into a pond near the town of Rendon. Emergency management reported roof damage at the fire station at 4416 Southwest Blvd in Fort Worth, TX. Emergency management reported trees down throughout the city of Mansfield, TX. A social media report indicated roof damage at Household of Faith Church in Arlington, TX. A social media report indicated downed communications antennas at Collins St and Sublett Rd in Arlington, TX. Broadcast media reported that one plane was damaged at Arlington Municipal Airport, and large trees were broken near the base of the trunks. A social media report indicated that several sections of brick wall were blown over near the intersection of Ponselle Dr and Matlock Rd in Arlington, TX. A 58 MPH wind gust was measured at the NWS office in Fort Worth, TX.

The following events were reported: Hail, Thunderstorm Wind, Tornado. These occurred near station USW00003927, approximately 31.721 km away, between 2017-03-29 00:50:00 and 2017-03-29 01:50:00. The events included records with hailstones measuring 56.56521739130435 inches.The events resulted in 0 injuries and 2 fatalities. The events caused property damage valued at 1235000.0 and crop damage of 0.0.
